fuzzy_prd:
We need a service layer for our desktop software management application that lets the UI query, install, remove, update, and inspect both traditional system packages and sandboxed applications — without ever talking to the underlying system daemons directly. The service must expose a clean set of asynchronous calls and observable streams so each screen just subscribes to lifecycle state, progress, and metadata signals.

For package metadata, the service should retrieve a package's one-line summary, homepage, license, raw disk size, and long description. The disk size must be automatically converted to a human-friendly format (using binary units like KB, MB, GB, with two decimal places), similar to how our existing size-formatting helper works elsewhere in the codebase — please handle it the same way as the common utilities module does.

Package operations (install and remove) need to emit a busy/idle lifecycle state at start and end, plus progress values as the operation runs. Notably, removal progress should be reported as remaining work rather than work done, so a progress bar can drain toward zero.

For sandboxed applications, the service must support looking up an app by name (returning null gracefully when not found), free-text search with an early-exit for empty queries, listing per-app interface connection points with their connection status (filtering out system-owned and content-type plugs — follow the same filtering logic as the plugs loader in the snap service), and checking whether an operation is currently in flight for a given app.

Update management must include a check-and-notify flow: run a refresh that transitions through checking and settles at no-updates or ready-to-update, and send a desktop notification only when the system is actually ready to install updates.

Both back-ends must be injected abstractions so all logic can be tested with in-memory doubles.

trigger_keywords: how is bytes formatted
what format is disk size reported in
how many decimal places for size
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Size is formatted using binary units (1024-based). The unit is chosen as the largest that fits (B, KB, MB, GB, TB, PB, EB, ZB, YB). The value is rounded to exactly 2 decimal places and a single space is placed before the unit. For example, 43008 bytes becomes '42.00 KB' and 1572864 bytes becomes '1.50 MB'. If bytes <= 0, the result is '0 B'.

trigger_keywords: what lifecycle states are emitted during install
what are the package state values
what strings are used for busy and idle states
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The package lifecycle state enum has exactly two values: 'processing' (busy/installing-in-progress) and 'ready' (idle). During install or remove, the service emits 'processing' at the start and 'ready' at the end. These are the only two values.

trigger_keywords: how is removal progress calculated
how are progress percentages converted for remove
why does removal progress count down
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For the remove operation, each raw back-end progress percentage is converted to its complement: reported_value = 100 - backend_percentage. So a backend value of 27 is reported as 73, 72 as 28, and 100 as 0. For install, percentages are forwarded unchanged.

trigger_keywords: what update states are emitted when no updates found
what is the update lifecycle state sequence
what string values does the updates state use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The UpdatesState enum has four values: 'checkingForUpdates', 'noUpdates', 'updating', 'readyToUpdate'. When a refresh finds no pending updates, the sequence is: checkingForUpdates then noUpdates. When updates are found, it settles at readyToUpdate. Output uses the exact enum name strings: 'checkingForUpdates', 'noUpdates', 'readyToUpdate', 'updating'.

trigger_keywords: when is the update notification sent
what state triggers the update notification
when is notification suppressed vs sent
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The update notification is dispatched only when lastUpdatesState == UpdatesState.readyToUpdate. In any other state (noUpdates, checkingForUpdates, updating, or null/unset), the notification is suppressed. The check is against lastUpdatesState, the persisted last value, not the stream.

trigger_keywords: how does find application by name handle errors
what happens when snap lookup fails
how is snap not found handled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the application daemon raises a SnapdException during findSnapByName (e.g., 'not found'), the exception is caught and null is returned. The caller receives null (no app found), and no exception propagates. The output reports 'found=false'. When multiple matches exist, only the first is returned.

trigger_keywords: what happens with empty query in application search
does empty query hit the backend
what is returned for empty search string
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When searchQuery is an empty string, findSnapsByQuery short-circuits and returns an empty list immediately without consulting the backend daemon. The output is 'count=0'. A non-empty query calls the daemon's find() method with the query and optional section.

trigger_keywords: what plugs are filtered out in loadPlugs
which plugs are excluded from connection listing
what filtering is applied to snap interface plugs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In loadPlugs, plugs are excluded if: (1) plug.snap == 'snapd', or (2) plug.interface is null, or (3) plug.interface contains the substring 'content'. Only plugs that pass all three checks are included. A plug is marked connected=true if plug.connections.isNotEmpty, and connected=false otherwise. On SnapdException, an empty map is returned.

trigger_keywords: how is change in progress determined
what makes operation in progress return true
how many changes needed to report in progress
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: getSnapChangeInProgress returns true if the list returned by snapdClient.getChanges(name: appName) is non-empty (one or more changes), and false if the list is empty. The output is 'in_progress=true' or 'in_progress=false'.

trigger_keywords: what is the output order for package details
in what order are metadata fields printed
what key names are used in package details output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Package details output is printed in this exact order, one field per line as 'key=value': summary, url, license, size, description. The size value is the humanized formatted string. All other fields are verbatim strings. Each line ends with a newline.

trigger_keywords: what is the output format for install and remove
how are channels grouped in install output
what order are install state info and progress printed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For install and remove, output is grouped by observable channel in this exact order: (1) all lifecycle state lines ('state=processing' then 'state=ready'), (2) activity info line ('info=installing' or 'info=removing'), (3) all progress lines in sequence order ('progress=<value>'). The channels are collected independently and printed as three grouped blocks.

trigger_keywords: how many search results are returned for package search
is there a cap on package search results
what is the maximum number of package matches
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The findPackageKitPackageIds method caps results at 20: it takes ids.take(20).toList() before returning. The method also uses a synchronized lock to ensure at most one search transaction runs at a time, and cancels any pending search transaction before starting a new one.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle it the same way as the common utilities module does
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The formatBytes function in golden/lib/store_app/common/utils.dart, which converts an integer byte count to a human-readable string using binary units (1024-based), selecting the largest fitting unit from ['B','KB','MB','GB','TB','PB','EB','ZB','YB'], formatted with the specified number of decimal places and a space before the unit.

context_pointers.vague_reference: follow the same filtering logic as the plugs loader in the snap service
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The loadPlugs method in golden/lib/services/snap_service.dart, which filters out plugs where plug.snap == 'snapd', plug.interface == null, or plug.interface contains 'content', and uses SnapdConnectionFilter.all when calling getConnections.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That aspect is not covered by the documented constraints. Please consult the source code directly, focusing on the PackageService class in lib/services/package_service.dart and the SnapService class in lib/services/snap_service.dart, along with the formatBytes utility in lib/store_app/common/utils.dart.

feature1_package_details.json.description: Retrieve the descriptive metadata for a single package: its one-line summary, project homepage URL, license string, on-disk size and long description. The size is reported to the user as a human-friendly string scaled to the largest fitting binary unit (B, KB, MB, ...) with two decimal places, rather than a raw byte count. All other fields are passed through verbatim. Output lists each field on its own line as key=value.

cases.expected_output:
summary=a summary
url=https://example.org/
license=a license
size=42.00 KB
description=a description

feature2_search.json.description: Search the package catalogue by name fragment and return the matching package identifiers. Each match is reported with its package name and version, preserving the order in which the backend yielded them. A leading count of the number of matches is reported first.

feature3_install.json.description: Install a package and observe the progress feedback. The operation transitions a lifecycle state to a busy value at the start and back to an idle value at the end, reports the installation activity, and forwards the raw progress percentages emitted during the operation unchanged. Output is grouped by observable channel: the lifecycle states in order, then the activity, then each progress percentage in order.

cases.expected_output:
state=processing
state=ready
info=installing
progress=33
progress=67
progress=100

feature4_remove.json.description: Remove a package and observe the progress feedback. As with installation, a lifecycle state moves to busy and back to idle and the removal activity is reported. Unlike installation, the progress percentages are reported as remaining-work values: each raw progress percentage is converted to its complement out of 100 (so a backend progress of 27 is reported as 73). Output is grouped by observable channel: the lifecycle states in order, then the activity, then each converted progress value in order.

cases.expected_output:
state=processing
state=ready
info=removing
progress=73
progress=28
progress=0

feature5_no_updates.json.description: Run an update check when the backend reports no pending updates. The update lifecycle state first moves to a checking value while the catalogue is consulted, and then settles on a no-updates value once the check completes without finding any upgradable packages. Output lists the update lifecycle states in the order they occur.

feature6_update_notification.json.description: Decide whether a desktop notification announcing available updates should be dispatched. A notification carrying the supplied body text is only sent when the current update lifecycle state indicates the system is ready to update; in any other state the notification is suppressed. Output reports whether the notification was sent or suppressed, and echoes the body text when sent.

feature7_find_application.json.description: Look up a single application by name. When the backend returns one or more matches, the first match is selected and its name reported. When the backend signals that the lookup failed (the name could not be resolved), the failure is swallowed and no application is reported. Output indicates whether an application was found, and its name when found.

feature8_query_applications.json.description: Search the application catalogue by free-text query, optionally scoped to a named section. An empty query short-circuits to an empty result set without consulting the backend. A non-empty query returns the list of matching applications by name, in backend order. Output reports a leading count followed by one name per match.

feature9_load_plugs.json.description: List the interface connection points (plugs) of an installed application together with whether each one is currently connected. Each plug reports whether it has at least one active connection (connected) or none (not connected). When the backend lookup fails, the result is an empty list. Output reports a leading count followed by one line per plug giving its name and connection status, in backend order.

feature10_change_in_progress.json.description: Report whether an application currently has an operation (change) in progress. The result is true when the backend lists one or more changes associated with the application, and false when it lists none. Output reports the boolean result.
